A 1010 foil usually gives you a really nice balance of:

• Easy pumping so you can connect waves
• Early lift which helps in knee to hip high surf
• Enough glide to keep speed through soft sections
• Still manageable at shoulder high without feeling too slow

Dropping to the 950 when the waves get a bit punchier makes sense. You normally get:

• More speed and tighter turning
• Less front wing drag
• Better control when the wave has more push
Very good balance of surfing on a wave and pumping

890 as the main wing for shoulder to head high waves makes a lot of sense. At that size you usually get:

• Much tighter turning radius
• More speed down the line
• Better control on steeper faces
• Less “over-foiling” feeling when the wave has power

And because you’re already an experienced foiler who likes linking waves and riding swell, the 890 is right in that sweet spot where it still pumps but feels really surfy.



1010
• Knee to waist high
• Weak / slow waves
• Maximum glide and pump for connecting waves

950
• Small but clean surf
• When you still want early lift but a bit more speed

890
• Shoulder to head high
• Best carving and most surf-like feeling

I mostly use the 780 for tow foiling at the moment and I really love it. It still holds a pretty good size of swell, feels super turny, and the glide is amazing as long as you keep the speed up. Especially on non breaking waves or rolling swell it’s insane.

I haven’t tried the 740 yet, but I did try the 830. For me the 830 just feels way too lifty. I would only use it in really small waves, like hip to maybe shoulder high fast waves. Otherwise it just feels like too much lift for what I like.

A few tips if you’re choosing a tow foil:

• Don’t go too big on the front wing. Bigger wings feel easier at the start but once the swell has energy they can feel really lifty and harder to control.
• Try to match the foil size to the speed of the swell, not just the wave height. Fast rolling swell usually feels better on a slightly smaller wing.
• Keep your speed up after you drop the rope. Smaller wings like the 780 really come alive when you carry speed and use the glide.
• If you want tighter turns and a more surfy feeling, going a little smaller on the front wing often makes a huge difference.

For me the 780 feels like a really nice balance between glide, speed, and turning.

I feel very in control at high speeds, and making carves feels very balanced. It turns super easy so I can stay high on the wave. The longer fuse is key for stability as well

For parawinging I use most of the time the 1010. It just gives you heaps of glide and makes the whole ride really easy, especially on longer runs where you want a bit of a break in between. You can just let it run and it keeps holding the swell without having to pump much.

If I’m doing shorter runs or want it to feel a bit more surfy and the swell is really cranking, then I go down to a 950 or even an 890. But most of the time I really enjoy the 1010 because it holds so much swell for downwinding on the Parawing. It gives you those little moments to relax between turns and just glide, which is super fun. I’m really loving that setup.

A few tips for others getting into Parawing downwinding:

• Don’t go too small on the foil at the start. A bit more surface like a 1300ish wing helps a lot with glide and connecting bumps.
• Focus on linking swell instead of pumping. Bigger wings make it easier to just read the ocean and flow from bump to bump.
• Choose the foil based on the energy of the swell. On strong windy days you can go smaller, but on weaker days the extra glide is gold.
• Let the foil work. Sometimes the best thing is to relax, keep your speed, and let the wing glide instead of forcing turns or pumps.

Once you get the feeling of just flowing with the swell, that’s when Parawinging becomes really addictive.

quiver_thoughts: Love it so much. Excited to hopefully get a tempo for downwinding after trying one last week, and also to try a smaller surge. I’m interested in trying the axis foil paddles because the smaller size is smaller than some other brands and I believe smaller blade size is better for that high cadence for take off.

RANGE. I've tried to have a very small quiver for my one SUP to surf and Downwind, but I wound up still having six foils. I switched to Axis in 2026 because the Surge is totally downwind able, 1010 for groveling and having to turn tight on very slow small waves and still pump paddle when needed. Then the 830 can surf from waist to overhead or Downwind in 20+ for my weight and still have glide and turn quick for fast decision making. Then the TEMPO covers at least two of my former downwind foils for the core of our majority Downwind which is 15-20 and mix of ground swell and confused bumps. I needed Moree time to make decisions and the TEMPO is starting to give that to me, and that has made a world of difference in my progressions. Before I was getting gassed if I took too long t find the next bump. At 51, this was a major upgrade!

Please get the Surge 1150 to the markest since I have sold my SF 1180 and ming out the light wind days with small bumps that was possible to ride with SF 1180😃. A Surf skinny 340 would be great for my size and weight with Surge 830.

Foiling is one of the most challenging watersports to learn, but it opens the door to a completely new dimension on the water. As a young and rapidly evolving sport, it keeps pushing the limits of what’s possible.

If you decide to start foiling, be ready to struggle at first. Progress doesn’t come easy. But the moment you rise above the water and start gliding silently on the foil, everything changes. Suddenly you feel weightless, connected to the water in a way that’s hard to describe.

That first real flight is unforgettable—and once you feel it, you’ll want more every single time you step on the board.
Make sure you have the right set up for the conditions you’re riding.
